body_txt: When the screaming infomercial finally reaches the point of telling you the price, you can be sure that price won't be a round number. Nothing will be sold for $10.00, $50.00, or $100.00. Every price will end with .95, .98, or .99, while larger prices (usually in the order of hundreds at the very least) will stop short of the nearest hundred and end up at 99, typically adding a smaller decimal for good measure. This trope isn't restricted to TV commercials . Real world pricing follows this trend as well, and has for a long time . Wikipedia refers to this as " psychological pricing ". This particular technique is known as "just-under pricing" and is a psychological tactic to make an item seem cheaper than it actually is. The premise is that people are usually mindless when doing their everyday business, and most advertisements and shopping places are specially designed to avoid having their customers become mindful and give as few cues as possible to wake up the viewer's consciousness; therefore, displaying a price of "$29.99" will, in theory, result in some people mistakenly reading "$20.00". In addition, making the most significant digit smaller (or removing an entire digit, if the price is an exact power of 10) also makes the price look less intimidating; "$9999", therefore, subconsciously looks smaller than "$10000" for the sole reason that the former price has 4 figures and the latter has 5. The principle is also applied in advertising, where a price of $29.99 can be claimed as "under $30!", and the repetition makes for audibly pleasing alliteration with suitably-enthusiastic narration: nine ninety-nine! or ninety-nine ninety-nine! This thinking is often carried over to large-ticket items, like cars, at least in the US — nobody cares about a few cents when they're spending five digits worth of dollars, but the MSRP (Manufacturer's Suggested Retail Price) in dollars will end in 7 or 5 far more often than it will end in 0. This trope gives another advantage for the retailer in countries like Japan, South Korea, Chile or Colombia, where one unit of their currency has a very small purchasing power (as of 9 September 2025, 1 USD can be respectively exchanged for 147 yen, 1389 won, 971 Chilean pesos, or 3956 Colombian pesos). Simply sell a pack of rice for $32,990 Colombian pesos (about 8 US dollars), receive $33,000 Colombian pesos as payment, pocket the whole money because the smallest coin is worth 50 COP and thus returning 10 COP of change is physically impossible, repeat 39 thousand times (which can easily happen in a single day if you're a big box supermarket), and voilà — 100 free US dollars worth of undelivered change. Another theory says this method of pricing was originally designed to prevent cashiers from pocketing payments. A price ending with .99 almost guarantees that the cashier will need to open the register to get change, which then logs the sale in the register. Some businesses also use this to keep track of sale items; normal prices end in .99, whereas sale prices end in, say, .79, and a clearance price is .59 — a useful shorthand if you have to check whether a hundred different prices have been switched back on the shelves when the sale is over. The trope has become so prevalent and ingrained that people automatically round prices up in their heads... even if it is a flat price. For instance, a person seeing $29.99 will immediately think "30 bucks", but if it's priced $29 flat, they may still round it up and think "30 bucks". On the other hand, said rounding up is a useful way to calculate whether you're still in your budget; rounding 29.50 down to "29 bucks" and being 50 cents over is a Very Bad Thing indeed. Other strange cent amounts can occur for many of the same reasons. Thrift shops may price things using a cents figure that is a repetition of the dollars figure to prevents customers from altering the prices by erasing the numbers — for instance, it would be blatantly obvious if someone tried changing a $13.13 price tag to $3.13.                 Country-specific examples Countries discontinuing coinage Many countries have phased out one cent coins (and sometimes two cents, or even anything below .1 currency unit), which are usually tiny and inconvenient and often cost more to make than they're worth, so prices under this trope sometimes change to be a multiple of the minimum coin denomination, like 95 cents. In other cases, the price only changes when a transaction is with paid with cash, automatically rounding the total to such a denomination, which can be up, down, or the nearest. note For example, in a country with 5c coins that uses nearest rounding, prices ending in 8, 9, 1 and 2 round to 0; and prices ending in 3, 4, 6 and 7 round to 5. . Since the rounding occurs after totaling up all the 99s or 95s, the psychological effect can sometimes remain the same.  Use of cents as marketing Aversions and change roundoffs
